title: CDC and snapshot input into an RDI pipeline
summary: Explains how committed source changes and initial snapshot reads become input to an RDI pipeline.

question_patterns: How does CDC produce new input?
What produces records for the pipeline?
How does snapshot input differ from CDC input?
Why is the collector behind the source?

nodes.label: Committed source changes
nodes.description: Insert, update, and delete operations committed to the source database.

nodes.label: Snapshot scan volume
nodes.description: The full source dataset that RDI reads during initial cache loading.

nodes.label: Collector source connection
nodes.description: Whether the collector can read source snapshot data and CDC changes.

nodes.label: Collector intake rate
nodes.description: The rate at which the collector accepts source records for the pipeline.

nodes.label: RDI stream append rate
nodes.description: The rate at which captured events are written to Redis streams in the RDI database.

nodes.label: RDI stream backlog
nodes.description: Captured events waiting in RDI streams to be processed, written to the target, and acknowledged.

nodes.label: Lag behind source
nodes.description: How far the pipeline is behind the source database in time.

edges.condition: CDC phase is active and the collector is connected to the source.
edges.summary: More committed source changes create more CDC input for the collector.
During initial cache loading, the full source dataset behaves like pipeline input.
A healthy source connection allows the collector to read snapshot rows and CDC events.

edges.condition: RDI has not throttled intake and the RDI database is available.
edges.summary: Higher collector intake increases the rate at which captured events are added to Redis streams.

edges.condition: Append rate exceeds completed processing rate.
edges.summary: Events appended to RDI streams increase backlog when they arrive faster than the stream processor completes them.

edges.condition: The source continues to receive changes while the collector cannot keep up.
edges.summary: Source changes increase lag when they are not captured and processed quickly enough.

causal_chains.question: How does CDC produce new input to the pipeline?
causal_chains.summary: In the CDC phase, committed source changes are captured by the collector, converted into pipeline input, and appended to Redis streams in the RDI database.

causal_chains.question: How does the snapshot phase differ from CDC for flow reasoning?
causal_chains.summary: During snapshot, the whole source dataset produces pipeline input. During CDC, newly committed source changes produce ongoing input. Both phases can create backlog if input exceeds processing capacity.

answering_guidance: CDC produces new pipeline input from committed source changes.
Snapshot produces input from the full selected source dataset.
If the collector is disconnected or throttled, source lag can increase even before events appear in RDI streams.
